narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 53.1% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 5 and 4 respectively, your LSAT score of 30, undergraduate GPA of 3.00, upper-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model focused on patterns from past students, zeroing in on a few key factors that most shaped its view of your bar exam prospects. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as it sits notably below the average of around 6 for those who passed, signaling to the model a potential dip in later academic momentum compared to successful peers. Next, your family income bracket played a supporting role in nudging the prediction toward failure, even though upper-middle is common among passers (about 45%), it didn't outweigh other concerns in your profile. On a more positive note, your LSAT score pulled in the opposite direction, helping to lower the failure risk since it showed some competitive strength relative to typical admitted students. Overall, these top factors—your Year 3 performance standing out most—meant your profile aligned more closely with students who struggled on the bar than those who succeeded, especially when your LSAT and GPA fell short of passers' averages of 37 and 3.24. While full-time status is a strong match for the 93% of successful students, it wasn't enough to shift the balance here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's just the model's data-driven read. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional coursework, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Many students in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded—reapplying after addressing these areas could make a real difference next time.
